Notes: Total community emissions: 274,584 MT CO2e. Down from 5.53 MT CO2e/person in 2005 (355,669 MT total). 2030 target: 2.51 MT CO2e/person (40% reduction below 1990 levels). Transportation accounts for 69% of emissions.
Source: City of Santa Cruz Climate Action Plan 2030; GHG Emissions Inventories

Geographic Scale: Santa Cruz County (monitoring station serves city)
Notes: Meets both US EPA standard (12 ug/m3) and WHO guideline (10 ug/m3 at time of measurement; WHO revised to 5 ug/m3 in 2021). 2017: 7.5 ug/m3; 2018: 8.5 ug/m3 (wildfire influenced). Avg 5.2 unhealthy PM2.5 days/year (2016-2018) vs federal allowance of 3.2.
Source: DataShare SCC / American Lung Association; IQAir

Notes: City provides 1,300+ acres of open space plus 696 acres across 43 parks (total ~2,000 acres). City total area: ~10,112 acres (15.8 sq mi). County-wide: 32% protected. Global biodiversity hotspot with 1,000+ native plant species and 35 endemic species.
Source: City of Santa Cruz Parks & Recreation; Santa Cruz County RCIS (2022)

Indicator: Nitrogen leached to groundwater per year
Data Point: Data not available at city level
Notes: City water is 95% surface water (San Lorenzo River, North Coast creeks, Loch Lomond reservoir), 5% groundwater from Live Oak wells. Pajaro Valley (south county) has significant nitrate contamination (87% from agriculture). City's watershed has lower agricultural intensity but septic systems in some areas may contribute.
Source: City of Santa Cruz Water Dept; Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency

Notes: County-level data from CA Dept of Pesticide Regulation. 67% of pesticides applied are fumigant gases - highest proportion of any CA county. City of Santa Cruz itself has minimal agricultural land; pesticide use is concentrated in south county (Pajaro Valley agriculture). City-specific data not separately reported.
Source: CA Department of Pesticide Regulation (CDPR) Pesticide Use Reports

Indicator: Percent of waste sent to landfill
Notes: County achieved 75% diversion rate (set as milestone in 2005, met by 2010). County has Zero Waste long-term goal. City operates Resource Recovery Facility at 605 Dimeo Lane. SB 1383 (2022) requires organics curbside collection. City-specific diversion rate not separately published vs county rate.
Source: Santa Cruz County Public Works; City of Santa Cruz Resource Recovery
